---
title: "Cell-Cell Communication Circle Plot"
author:
  - "**[Editor]** [Bizard Team](https://github.com/openbiox/Bizard);"
  - "**[Contributors]** [ShiXiang Wang](https://github.com/ShixiangWang)."
---

The Cell-Cell Communication Circle Plot (细胞-细胞通讯网络圈图) is a specialized visualization for depicting intercellular signaling interactions inferred from single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data. Using the **CellChat** R package, this plot presents a circular network where nodes represent cell populations (cell types or clusters) and directed edges indicate the strength and direction of ligand-receptor communication signals between them.

The arc width of each edge encodes communication probability, and the self-loops on each node represent autocrine signaling. Color coding maps each cell type to a distinct hue, and arrow directionality shows the sender-receiver relationship. This visualization is particularly powerful for discovering cross-talk between immune cells and tumor cells, identifying key signaling hubs in tissue microenvironments, and comparing communication networks across biological conditions.

## Example

![](../images/Omics/CellChatCirclePlot_demo.png){fig-alt="Cell-Cell Communication Circle Plot DEMO" fig-align="center" width="60%"}

## Setup

-   System Requirements: Cross-platform (Linux/MacOS/Windows)

-   Programming Language: R

-   Dependencies: `CellChat`, `ggplot2`, `patchwork`, `Seurat` (optional, for loading scRNA-seq data)

```{r packages setup, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, output=FALSE}
# Install Bioconductor dependencies first
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("BiocManager")
}

# Install NMF and other CRAN dependencies
if (!requireNamespace("NMF", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("NMF")
}
if (!requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("ggplot2")
}
if (!requireNamespace("patchwork", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("patchwork")
}
if (!requireNamespace("igraph", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("igraph")
}

# Install CellChat from GitHub
if (!requireNamespace("CellChat", quietly = TRUE)) {
  if (!requireNamespace("devtools", quietly = TRUE)) {
    install.packages("devtools")
  }
  devtools::install_github("jinworks/CellChat")
}

# Load packages
library(CellChat)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
```

```{r session info}
sessioninfo::session_info("attached")
```

## Data Preparation

We use the built-in example dataset from the CellChat package, which contains a processed single-cell RNA-seq dataset of human skin cells at wound healing stages. This dataset has pre-computed cell-cell communication results ready for visualization.

```{r load data, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# For demonstration, we load CellChat's built-in example object
# In practice, you would have built a CellChat object from your own scRNA-seq data
cellchat <- createCellChat(
  object = CellChat::normalHuman,   # built-in scRNA-seq count matrix (subset)
  meta = CellChat::normalHumanMeta, # cell-type metadata
  group.by = "labels"               # column specifying cell type labels
)
```

::: callout-note
If `CellChat::normalHuman` is not available in your CellChat version, replace the above with loading a pre-built CellChat RDS object or build one from your own Seurat object as shown in the "Upstream Workflow" section below.
:::

### Alternative: Build CellChat Object from Scratch (Reference)

The following code is for reference only and will not be executed. It demonstrates how to build a CellChat object from a Seurat single-cell dataset before making communication plots.

```{r upstream workflow}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true

library(Seurat)
library(CellChat)

# Step 1: Prepare the count matrix and metadata from a Seurat object
seurat_obj <- readRDS("your_seurat_object.rds")
data_input <- GetAssayData(seurat_obj, assay = "RNA", slot = "data") # normalized counts
meta       <- seurat_obj@meta.data

# Step 2: Create a CellChat object
cellchat <- createCellChat(object = data_input, meta = meta, group.by = "cell_type")

# Step 3: Set the ligand-receptor interaction database
CellChatDB <- CellChatDB.human  # use CellChatDB.mouse for mouse data
cellchat@DB <- CellChatDB

# Step 4: Pre-process the expression data
cellchat <- subsetData(cellchat)         # subset to expressed signaling genes
cellchat <- identifyOverExpressedGenes(cellchat)
cellchat <- identifyOverExpressedInteractions(cellchat)

# Step 5: Infer communication probabilities
cellchat <- computeCommunProb(cellchat, type = "triMean")
cellchat <- filterCommunication(cellchat, min.cells = 10)

# Step 6: Infer signaling pathways
cellchat <- computeCommunProbPathway(cellchat)
cellchat <- aggregateNet(cellchat)
```

### Load Pre-built CellChat Example Object

For this tutorial, we download a pre-built CellChat object from the Bizard data repository so the tutorial remains self-contained and reproducible.

```{r load example cellchat, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Download pre-built CellChat example object
cellchat_rds_url <- "https://bizard-1301043367.cos.ap-guangzhou.myqcloud.com/CellChat_example.rds"
local_rds <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds")

if (!file.exists(local_rds)) {
  tryCatch(
    download.file(cellchat_rds_url, local_rds, quiet = TRUE),
    error = function(e) message("Download failed, using simulation data")
  )
}

# If download succeeded, load the object; otherwise build a minimal simulation
if (file.exists(local_rds) && file.size(local_rds) > 1000) {
  cellchat <- readRDS(local_rds)
} else {
  # Fallback: build a minimal CellChat-like net matrix for demonstration
  message("Using simulated communication matrix for demonstration")
  
  # Simulate a communication count matrix (cell types x cell types)
  cell_types <- c("CD4 T", "CD8 T", "NK", "B cell", "Monocyte",
                  "DC", "Fibroblast", "Epithelial", "Endothelial")
  n <- length(cell_types)
  set.seed(42)
  
  # Create a fake aggregated net list that CellChat's netVisual_circle expects
  net_count  <- matrix(sample(0:50,  n * n, replace = TRUE), n, n,
                       dimnames = list(cell_types, cell_types))
  net_weight <- matrix(runif(n * n, 0, 1), n, n,
                       dimnames = list(cell_types, cell_types))
  diag(net_count)  <- 0
  diag(net_weight) <- 0
  
  # Store in a minimal list (mimics cellchat@net)
  cellchat_net <- list(count = net_count, weight = net_weight)
}
```

## Visualization

### 1. Circle Plot of Overall Cell-Cell Communication Network

The overall circle plot summarizes all inferred communication interactions. Each node represents a cell type; the size of the node is proportional to the total number of interactions that cell type participates in. The arc width encodes communication strength (interaction weight).

```{r fig1-overall-circle, fig.width=8, fig.height=8, warning=FALSE}
#| label: fig1-overall-circle
#| fig-cap: "Overall Cell-Cell Communication Circle Plot"
#| out.width: "80%"

if (inherits(cellchat, "CellChat")) {
  # For a real CellChat object
  groupSize <- as.numeric(table(cellchat@idents))
  
  par(mfrow = c(1, 2), xpd = TRUE)
  
  # Panel A: Number of interactions
  netVisual_circle(
    cellchat@net$count,
    vertex.weight    = groupSize,
    weight.scale     = TRUE,
    label.edge       = FALSE,
    title.name       = "Number of interactions"
  )
  
  # Panel B: Interaction strength (weights)
  netVisual_circle(
    cellchat@net$weight,
    vertex.weight    = groupSize,
    weight.scale     = TRUE,
    label.edge       = FALSE,
    title.name       = "Interaction weights/strength"
  )
  
  par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
} else {
  # Fallback: use the simulated matrix
  groupSize <- rowSums(cellchat_net$count)
  
  par(mfrow = c(1, 2), xpd = TRUE)
  
  netVisual_circle(
    cellchat_net$count,
    vertex.weight    = groupSize,
    weight.scale     = TRUE,
    label.edge       = FALSE,
    title.name       = "Number of interactions"
  )
  
  netVisual_circle(
    cellchat_net$weight,
    vertex.weight    = groupSize,
    weight.scale     = TRUE,
    label.edge       = FALSE,
    title.name       = "Interaction weights/strength"
  )
  
  par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
}
```

### 2. Circle Plot for Individual Cell Groups

To focus on a specific cell type and its outgoing or incoming communications, we can subset the network matrix to highlight that cell type.

```{r fig2-individual-circle, fig.width=10, fig.height=10, warning=FALSE}
#| label: fig2-individual-circle
#| fig-cap: "Cell-Specific Communication Circle Plot"
#| out.width: "90%"

if (inherits(cellchat, "CellChat")) {
  mat  <- cellchat@net$weight
  groupSize <- as.numeric(table(cellchat@idents))
} else {
  mat  <- cellchat_net$weight
  groupSize <- rowSums(cellchat_net$count)
}

n_types <- nrow(mat)
n_cols  <- ceiling(sqrt(n_types))
n_rows  <- ceiling(n_types / n_cols)

par(mfrow = c(n_rows, n_cols), xpd = TRUE, mar = c(1, 1, 2, 1))

for (i in seq_len(n_types)) {
  mat_i <- matrix(0, nrow = n_types, ncol = n_types,
                  dimnames = dimnames(mat))
  mat_i[i, ] <- mat[i, ]   # outgoing signals from cell type i
  
  netVisual_circle(
    mat_i,
    vertex.weight = groupSize,
    weight.scale  = TRUE,
    edge.weight.max = max(mat),
    title.name    = rownames(mat)[i]
  )
}

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

### 3. Signaling Pathway-Specific Circle Plot

When a real CellChat object is available, we can visualize communication for specific signaling pathways (e.g., MHC-II, CXCL, CCL). This reveals which cell types are the dominant senders and receivers for a particular pathway.

```{r fig3-pathway-circle, fig.width=7, fig.height=7, warning=FALSE}
#| label: fig3-pathway-circle
#| fig-cap: "Signaling Pathway Circle Plot (MHC-II pathway)"
#| out.width: "70%"

if (inherits(cellchat, "CellChat") &&
    length(cellchat@netP$pathways) > 0) {
  
  # Choose the first available pathway for demonstration
  pathway_show <- cellchat@netP$pathways[1]
  message("Visualizing pathway: ", pathway_show)
  
  netVisual_aggregate(
    cellchat,
    signaling    = pathway_show,
    layout       = "circle",
    edge.weight.max = NULL
  )
} else {
  message("No signaling pathway data available in the loaded object.")
  message("With a complete CellChat object, use:")
  message('  netVisual_aggregate(cellchat, signaling = "MHC-II", layout = "circle")')
  
  # Show an annotated placeholder with simulated data
  if (exists("cellchat_net")) {
    par(xpd = TRUE)
    netVisual_circle(
      cellchat_net$weight,
      vertex.weight = rowSums(cellchat_net$count),
      weight.scale  = TRUE,
      title.name    = "Simulated pathway view"
    )
  }
}
```

### 4. Chord Diagram Variant

CellChat also supports chord diagram visualization (`layout = "chord"`), which is an alternative representation of the same circle plot data.

```{r fig4-chord, fig.width=7, fig.height=7, warning=FALSE}
#| label: fig4-chord
#| fig-cap: "Cell-Cell Communication Chord Diagram"
#| out.width: "70%"

if (inherits(cellchat, "CellChat") && length(cellchat@netP$pathways) > 0) {
  netVisual_aggregate(
    cellchat,
    signaling = cellchat@netP$pathways[1],
    layout    = "chord"
  )
} else if (exists("cellchat_net")) {
  # Fallback: draw a basic chord using the circlize library
  if (!requireNamespace("circlize", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("circlize")
  library(circlize)
  
  chordDiagram(
    cellchat_net$weight,
    transparency = 0.5,
    annotationTrack = "grid",
    preAllocateTracks = 1
  )
  circos.trackPlotRegion(
    track.index = 1,
    panel.fun  = function(x, y) {
      circos.text(
        CELL_META$xcenter,
        ylim()[1] + .1,
        CELL_META$sector.index,
        facing    = "clockwise",
        niceFacing = TRUE,
        adj       = c(0, 0.5),
        cex       = 0.7
      )
    },
    bg.border = NA
  )
  title("Cell-Cell Communication (Chord Diagram)")
  circos.clear()
}
```

## Advanced: Heatmap and Bubble Plot Views

Beyond circle plots, CellChat provides complementary heatmap and bubble plot views of the same communication data.

```{r fig5-heatmap, fig.width=8, fig.height=6, warning=FALSE}
#| label: fig5-heatmap
#| fig-cap: "Cell-Cell Communication Heatmap"
#| out.width: "80%"

if (inherits(cellchat, "CellChat")) {
  netVisual_heatmap(cellchat, measure = "weight", color.heatmap = "Reds")
} else if (exists("cellchat_net")) {
  library(ggplot2)
  df_heat <- as.data.frame(as.table(cellchat_net$weight))
  colnames(df_heat) <- c("Sender", "Receiver", "Weight")
  
  ggplot(df_heat, aes(x = Receiver, y = Sender, fill = Weight)) +
    geom_tile(color = "white") +
    scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "#b74147", name = "Weight") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(
      axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
      panel.grid  = element_blank()
    ) +
    labs(
      title = "Cell-Cell Communication Heatmap",
      x = "Receiver", y = "Sender"
    )
}
```

## References

\[1\] Jin S, Guerrero-Juarez CF, Zhang L, et al. Inference and analysis of cell-cell communication using CellChat. *Nature Communications*. 2021;12(1):1088. <https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-021-21246-9>

\[2\] Jin S, et al. CellChat for systematic analysis of cell-cell communication from single-cell transcriptomics. *Nature Protocols*. 2024. <https://doi.org/10.1038/s41596-024-01045-4>

\[3\] CellChat GitHub: <https://github.com/jinworks/CellChat>
